steps.title: Identify the specific claim to illustrate
steps.content: The passage says Aristophanes satirizes sophists as teaching people to speak dishonestly. The correct quotation must show a character being taught—or wanting to learn—dishonest speech directly from a sophist. A quotation that merely shows enthusiasm for sophistry, desire for wealth, or memory training will not do.

steps.title: Eliminate choices that miss the dishonesty claim, then confirm the match
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Strepsiades encourages his son to learn to be a sophist, saying, "If you have any concern for your father's patrimony, become one of them."': Urges joining sophists for financial reasons—no mention of dishonest speech. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Pheidippides says, after taking lessons from a sophist, "How pleasant it is to be acquainted with new and clever things, and to be able to despise the established laws!"': Celebrates cleverness and lawlessness, not specifically the instruction of dishonest speaking. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Strepsiades, taking lessons from a sophist, says he wants to become "a fabricator of falsehoods, inventive of words, a practiced knave in lawsuits.... a fox, a sharper, a slippery knave, a dissembler, a slippery fellow, an impostor."': A student mid-lesson explicitly lists dishonest speaking skills ("fabricator of falsehoods," "dissembler")—directly illustrating sophists teaching dishonest speech. ✓
  • Choice D — 'Socrates, a sophist, says to a potential customer, "I wish to briefly learn from you if you are possessed of a good memory."': Shows a sophist asking about memory—no connection to dishonest speech. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
